perturbation.original_text: (b) If, as of the end of any period of the immediately previous four consecutive Agreement Quarters, Promotion Net Sales for such period are less than $[***], either party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement on 120 days' prior written notice to the other party, which notice may not be given before the third anniversary of the Promotion Commencement Date.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) If, as of the end of any period of the immediately previous four consecutive Agreement Quarters, Promotion Net Sales for such period are less than $[***], either party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement on 15 days' prior written notice to the other party, which notice may not be given before the third anniversary of the Promotion Commencement Date.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notice period to 15 days could be problematic, as some jurisdictions require longer notice periods to allow for transition.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification Act (WARN)
perturbation.law_explanation: The WARN Act requires 60 days' notice for plant closings and mass layoffs. While this is a contract termination clause, a sudden termination could trigger mass layoffs, and 15 days' notice is insufficient under federal law where the WARN act applies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: U.S.C. Title 29 - LABOR 29 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 29 - LABOR CHAPTER 23 - WORKER ADJUSTMENT AND RETRAINING NOTIFICATION Sec. 2102 - Notice required before plant closings and mass layoffs From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §2102. Notice required before plant closings and mass layoffs (a) Notice to employees, State dislocated worker units, and local governments An employer shall not order a plant closing or mass layoff until the end of a 60-day period after the employer serves written notice of such an order— (1) to each representative of the affected employees as of the time of the notice or, if there is no such representative at that time, to each affected employee; and (2) to the State or entity designated by the State to carry out rapid response activities under section 2864(a)(2)(A) of this title, and the chief elected official of the unit of local government within which such closing or layoff is to occur. If there is more than one such unit, the unit of local government which the employer shall notify is the unit of local government to which the employer pays the highest taxes for the year preceding the year for which the determination is made. (b) Reduction of notification period (1) An employer may order the shutdown of a single site of employment before the conclusion of the 60-day period if as of the time that notice would have been required the employer was actively seeking capital or business which, if obtained, would have enabled the employer to avoid or postpone the shutdown and the employer reasonably and in good faith believed that giving the notice required would have precluded the employer from obtaining the needed capital or business. (2)(A) An employer may order a plant closing or mass layoff before the conclusion of the 60-day period if the closing or mass layoff is caused by business circumstances that were not reasonably foreseeable as of the time that notice would have been required. (B) No notice under this chapter shall be required if the plant closing or mass layoff is due to any form of natural disaster, such as a flood, earthquake, or the droug

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notice period for contract termination from 120 days to 15 days. The Law Explanation points out the WARN Act requires 60 days' notice for plant closings and mass layoffs. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states, "An employer shall not order a plant closing or mass layoff until the end of a 60-day period after the employer serves written notice of such an order." The change in the contract directly contradicts this requirement if the termination results in a plant closing or mass layoff, making it an Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation also highlights that shorter notice periods could be problematic in some jurisdictions.

perturbation.original_text: Section 5.6 Adverse Drug Experience Reports. (a) Each party shall notify the other: (i) of all Serious Adverse Drug Experience Reports within forty-eight (48) hours of the time such Serious Adverse Drug Experience Report becomes known to such party (including its employees); and (ii) of all Adverse Drug Experience Reports within five (5) calendar days of the time such Adverse Drug Experience Report becomes known to such party (including its employees).
perturbation.changed_text: Section 5.6 Adverse Drug Experience Reports. (a) Each party shall notify the other: (i) of all Serious Adverse Drug Experience Reports within seventy-two (72) hours of the time such Serious Adverse Drug Experience Report becomes known to such party (including its employees); and (ii) of all Adverse Drug Experience Reports within ten (10) calendar days of the time such Adverse Drug Experience Report becomes known to such party (including its employees).
perturbation.explanation: The FDA requires expedited reporting of serious adverse events within a specific timeframe. Delaying this notification could violate FDA regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: This regulation dictates the postmarketing reporting of adverse drug experiences. The contract's modified timeline extends the reporting timeframe, which could cause failure to report within the time frame required by the FDA.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract extends the reporting timelines for Serious Adverse Drug Experience Reports from 48 to 72 hours, and for all Adverse Drug Experience Reports from 5 to 10 calendar days. This creates an inconsistency with potential FDA regulations that mandate expedited reporting, as highlighted by the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. The snippet, "eCFR :: 21 CFR 314.80 -- Postmarketing reporting of adverse drug experiences," indicates the existence of federal regulations governing the timelines for reporting adverse drug experiences, thus supporting the existence of a contradiction because the changed text could cause a failure to report within the federally mandated timeframe. The Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition states that a contradiction occurs when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law.

perturbation.original_text: Within thirty (30) days following the end of each Agreement Quarter, King shall provide the JCC with a status report, which report will summarize King's Promotional activities pursuant to this Agreement for such prior Agreement Quarter and on a calendar year-to-date basis, including, to the extent King customarily creates the following reports for King's other products which are promoted by or on behalf of King: (a) the number of P1, P2 and P3 Details made and recorded by King's standard record keeping procedures; (b) the names and addresses of the Professionals called upon; (c) the percentage of Professionals Detailed who were provided with Samples; (d) the average number of such Samples delivered on each Detail; (e) a breakdown of all information required to be contained in each report on an aggregate basis; (f) any Professionals added to the King Physician List during such quarter; and (g) such other information as may be required in the then- current Annual Plan.
perturbation.changed_text: Within ninety (90) days following the end of each Agreement Quarter, King shall provide the JCC with a status report, which report will summarize King's Promotional activities pursuant to this Agreement for such prior Agreement Quarter and on a calendar year-to-date basis, including, to the extent King customarily creates the following reports for King's other products which are promoted by or on behalf of King: (a) the number of P1, P2 and P3 Details made and recorded by King's standard record keeping procedures; (b) the names and addresses of the Professionals called upon; (c) the percentage of Professionals Detailed who were provided with Samples; (d) the average number of such Samples delivered on each Detail; (e) a breakdown of all information required to be contained in each report on an aggregate basis; (f) any Professionals added to the King Physician List during such quarter; and (g) such other information as may be required in the then- current Annual Plan.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the reporting deadline to 90 days may cause inconsistencies with regulations that require more frequent reporting of marketing activities or sample distribution, particularly in states with stricter pharmaceutical marketing laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Though not directly specifying report timelines for *promotional* activities, the PDMA mandates stringent controls over drug sample distribution. States interpret and build upon this federal law to regulate pharmaceutical marketing, often requiring more frequent reporting of sample distribution than every 90 days. This modification contradicts the spirit and potential state-level implementations of the PDMA's sample accountability requirements.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) | FDA Skip to main content Skip to FDA Search Skip to in this section menu Skip to footer links In this section : Laws Enforced by FDA Home Regulatory Information Laws Enforced by FDA Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) Laws Enforced by FDA FD&C Act Reference Information United States Code, Title 21 The laws of the United States are organized by subject into the United States Code. The United States Code contains only the currently enacted statutory language. The official United States Code is maintained by the Office of the Law Revision Counsel in the United States House of Representatives. The Office of the Law Revision Counsel reviews enacted laws and determines where the statutory language should be codified related to its topic. The Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act and subsequent amending statutes are codified into Title 21 Chapter 9 of the United States Code. The listing of FD&C Act sections presented here identifies both the FD&C Act and U.S. Code section numbers, which can be used to narrow your search on the Law Revision Counsel website. To search the FD&C Act on the Law Revision Counsel website, use the Advanced Search to limit the your search to Title 21. To search the FD&C Act on the Law Revision Counsel website, you may either search by U.S. code section number or browse the Title 21 section listing . Content current as of: 03/29/2018 Back to Top
perturbation.justification: The change from 30 to 90 days for reporting promotional activities creates a potential contradiction with state-level implementations of the PDMA's sample accountability requirements. The Law Explanation states that states often require more frequent reporting than every 90 days, building upon the federal law (PDMA). While neither scraped snippet directly specifies report timelines, Snippet 2 references the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act), implying that more specific regulations, potentially addressing reporting frequency, exist within the broader framework of food and drug laws.
